---
title: "Client Report -How good is it, really?"
subtitle: "Unit 4 Task 2"
author: "Vincent Gentry"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import polars as pl
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn import metrics
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.inspection import permutation_importance

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)
```


```{python}
url = "https://github.com/byuidatascience/data4dwellings/raw/master/data-raw/dwellings_ml/dwellings_ml.csv"
dwellings = pl.read_csv(url)

features = [c for c in dwellings.columns if c not in ("parcel", "yrbuilt", "before1980")]
X = dwellings.select(features).to_numpy()
y = dwellings["before1980"].to_numpy()

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.30, random_state=42)

clf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200, random_state=42)
clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred = clf.predict(X_test)
y_prob = clf.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]
```

## QUESTION 1

1. __Describe the quality of your classification model using 2-3 different evaluation metrics.__ You also need to explain how to define/interpret each of the evaluation metrics you use.  

I used three different ways to check how well my model is doing:

- **Accuracy** – This is just the percentage of homes the model guessed right. My model got about **93%**, which means it only got about 7 out of 100 homes wrong. That's pretty solid.
- **Precision and Recall** – These come from the classification report. Precision tells you when the model says a house is "Before 1980", how often is it actually right? Recall tells you, out of all the houses that really were built before 1980, how many did the model actually catch? The F1-score combines both into one number, which is nice when you want a quick summary.
- **ROC-AUC** – This one was new to me. It basically measures how good the model is at telling the two groups apart. A score of 1.0 means perfect, and 0.5 means the model is just guessing randomly. My model scored around **0.98**, which means it's doing a really good job separating pre-1980 homes from newer ones.

```{python}
# Include and execute your code here
print(f"Accuracy: {metrics.accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred):.4f}\n")
print(metrics.classification_report(y_test, y_pred, target_names=["1980 or Later", "Before 1980"]))
print(f"ROC-AUC:  {metrics.roc_auc_score(y_test, y_prob):.4f}")

# Confusion matrix plot
cm = metrics.confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred)
cm_data = {
    "Actual":    ["1980 or Later", "1980 or Later", "Before 1980", "Before 1980"],
    "Predicted": ["1980 or Later", "Before 1980",  "1980 or Later", "Before 1980"],
    "Count":     [int(cm[0,0]), int(cm[0,1]), int(cm[1,0]), int(cm[1,1])],
}
(
    ggplot(cm_data, aes(x="Predicted", y="Actual", fill="Count"))
    + geom_tile()
    + geom_text(aes(label="Count"), color="white", size=14)
    + scale_fill_gradient(low="#3182bd", high="#08519c")
    + labs(title="Confusion Matrix")
)
```


## QUESTION 2

1. __Justify your classification model by discussing the most important features selected by your model.__ This discussion should include a plot of permutation importance values for the most important features. Were there any features you thought would be importance but weren't?

To figure out which features actually matter, I used permutation importance. The idea is if you randomly shuffle a column and the model gets a lot worse, that column must be important. If nothing changes, it probably isn't.

The top features turned out to be **`livearea`**, **`sprice`**, and **`arcstyle_ONE-STORY`**, which are the same three I guessed would be important back in Task 1, so that was cool to see confirmed. I actually thought `stories` and `numbaths` would rank higher since those seem like they'd change over time, but the model didn't find them very useful — probably because you can find both old and new homes with those same values.

```{python}
# Include and execute your code here
perm = permutation_importance(clf, X_test, y_test, n_repeats=10, random_state=42, n_jobs=1)

imp_df = (
    pl.DataFrame({"feature": features, "importance": perm.importances_mean.tolist()})
    .sort("importance", descending=True)
    .head(15)
)

(
    ggplot(
        {"feature": imp_df["feature"].to_list(), "importance": imp_df["importance"].to_list()},
        aes(x="feature", y="importance")
    )
    + geom_bar(stat="identity", fill="#2171b5")
    + coord_flip()
    + labs(x="Feature", y="Mean Decrease in Accuracy", title="Top 15 Permutation Importances")
)
```

## QUESTION 3

1. __For the 3 most important features, explain their relationship to the target variable. Use a plot to illustrate the relationship.__ (3 plots minimum)

Looking at the three most important features, you can really see why the model picks up on them:

1. **`livearea`** – Older homes are just smaller. Pre-1980 homes are usually around 1,280 sq ft while newer homes are closer to 1,860 sq ft. That's a pretty big difference the model can use.
2. **`sprice`** – Old homes sell for a lot less than new ones. This makes sense since newer homes are bigger and in better condition, so they command higher prices.
3. **`arcstyle_ONE-STORY`** – A lot of older homes are simple one-story ranch-style houses. That style was super common before 1980 but less so for newer builds, so it's a decent clue about age.

```{python}
# Include and execute your code here
era_label = ["Before 1980" if v == 1 else "1980 or Later" for v in dwellings["before1980"].to_list()]

# Plot 1: livearea
p1 = (
    ggplot(
        {"livearea": dwellings["livearea"].to_list(), "era": era_label},
        aes(x="era", y="livearea", fill="era")
    )
    + geom_boxplot(outlier_size=1)
    + labs(x="Era", y="Living Area (sq ft)", title="Living Area by Era")
    + theme(legend_position="none")
)

# Plot 2: sprice
p2 = (
    ggplot(
        {"sprice": dwellings["sprice"].to_list(), "era": era_label},
        aes(x="era", y="sprice", fill="era")
    )
    + geom_boxplot(outlier_size=1)
    + labs(x="Era", y="Sale Price ($)", title="Sale Price by Era")
    + theme(legend_position="none")
)

# Plot 3: arcstyle_ONE-STORY proportion
one_story_pct = [
    dwellings.filter(pl.col("before1980") == 1)["arcstyle_ONE-STORY"].mean(),
    dwellings.filter(pl.col("before1980") == 0)["arcstyle_ONE-STORY"].mean(),
]
p3 = (
    ggplot(
        {"era": ["Before 1980", "1980 or Later"], "pct": one_story_pct},
        aes(x="era", y="pct", fill="era")
    )
    + geom_bar(stat="identity")
    + scale_y_continuous(limits=[0, 1])
    + labs(x="Era", y="Proportion One-Story", title="One-Story Homes by Era")
    + theme(legend_position="none")
)

gggrid([p1, p2, p3], ncol=3)
```
